title: The Shoe Repairman's Radio
text_zh: 街角那个修鞋的摊子已经在那里二十多年了，摊主老吴的标配是一台老式收音机，永远调在同一个频率——某个播评书的电台。不管手里的活多忙，老吴都不会关掉收音机。他说安静会让他分心，反而是有人在耳边说故事的时候，手上的动作最稳。我观察过他工作的样子：低着头，嘴角微微上扬，针线在手指间穿梭如蛇，偶尔评书讲到精彩处他会停一下，抬头看一眼收音机，好像能从那个小喇叭里看到画面似的。有一天我去取修好的皮鞋，正赶上收音机没电了。老吴坐在那里发愣，手里的鞋放了好一会儿都没动。他有些不好意思地说：「没有声音，手就不听使唤了。」我帮他买了电池回来，评书声重新响起的瞬间，他的手指立刻恢复了那种从容的节奏。那一刻我忽然想到，所谓匠人精神也许不是什么高深的东西，它可能就是一个人用二十年时间建立起来的一套极其私密的工作仪式——一台收音机、一个频率、一种背景声，缺了任何一个环节，整个系统就会停摆。
text_pinyin: Jiejiao na ge xiu xie de tanzi yijing zai nali ershi duo nian le, tanzhu Lao Wu de biaopei shi yi tai laoshi shouyinji, yongyuan tiao zai tong yi ge pinlv—mou ge bo pingshu de diantai. Buguan shou li de huo duo mang, Lao Wu dou bu hui guandiao shouyinji. Ta shuo anjing hui rang ta fenxin, fan'er shi you ren zai erbian shuo gushi de shihou, shou shang de dongzuo zui wen. Wo guancha guo ta gongzuo de yangzi: di zhe tou, zuijiao weiwei shangyang, zhenxian zai shouzhi jian chuansuo ru she, ou'er pingshu jiang dao jingcai chu ta hui ting yi xia, taitou kan yi yan shouyinji, haoxiang neng cong nage xiao laba li kan dao huamian shi de. You yi tian wo qu qu xiu hao de pixie, zheng gan shang shouyinji mei dian le. Lao Wu zuo zai nali faleng, shou li de xie fang le hao yihuir dou mei dong. Ta youxie bu hao yisi de shuo: 'Meiyou shengyin, shou jiu bu ting shihuanle.' Wo bang ta mai le dianchi huilai, pingshu sheng chongxin xiangqi de shunjian, ta de shouzhi like huifu le na zhong congrong de jiezou. Na yi ke wo huran xiangdao, suowei jiangren jingshen yexu bu shi shenme gaoshen de dongxi, ta keneng jiu shi yi ge ren yong ershi nian shijian jianli qilai de yi tao jiqi simi de gongzuo yishi—yi tai shouyinji, yi ge pinlv, yi zhong beijing sheng, que le renhe yi ge huanjie, zhenggege xitong jiu hui tingbai.
text_en: The shoe repair stand on the street corner has been there for over twenty years. The proprietor Old Wu's standard equipment is a vintage radio, always tuned to the same frequency—a station that broadcasts storytelling. No matter how busy his work, Old Wu never turns off the radio. He says silence makes him distracted; rather, when someone is telling stories in his ear, his hands are steadiest. I've watched him work: head lowered, corners of his mouth slightly upturned, needle and thread shuttling through his fingers like a snake, occasionally pausing at an exciting part of the story to glance up at the radio, as if he could see images through that little speaker. One day I went to pick up my repaired leather shoes just as the radio ran out of batteries. Old Wu sat there in a daze, the shoe in his hand untouched for quite a while. He said somewhat sheepishly: 'Without sound, my hands won't obey.' I bought batteries for him, and the instant the storytelling resumed, his fingers immediately recovered their composed rhythm. In that moment I suddenly thought: what we call the artisan spirit may not be anything profound—it might just be an extremely private work ritual that a person builds over twenty years: a radio, a frequency, a background sound. Remove any single element and the entire system stalls.

title: The Translator's Margin Notes
text_zh: 在旧书店淘到一本七十年代出版的契诃夫短篇小说集，译者是我从未听说过的名字。书的状态不算好，封面卷角，书脊开裂，但真正吸引我的是满满的页边笔记。笔迹工整而细小，用的是那个年代特有的蓝黑色钢笔墨水。笔记的内容让我着迷——不是普通读者的感想，而是关于翻译本身的反复斟酌。比如在一个描写黄昏的段落旁边，译者写道：「此处原文用了一个包含光影渐变之意的俄文词，中文里没有对等物。试过'暮色渐沉'，太文雅；'天慢慢黑了'，太白话。最终选了'光线一层层剥落'，虽然不够忠实但保留了原文的动态感。」这种翻译的挣扎在每一页上都有痕迹。更打动我的是书末的一段话：「翻译就是在两种语言之间走钢丝，左边是忠实，右边是自然，而你永远在失去平衡。唯一的安慰是偶尔某个句子会自己找到它的中文模样，那一刻你只是抄写员，不是翻译。」这位无名译者恐怕一辈子都默默无闻，但他在页边留下的思考痕迹，比很多著名翻译家的序言都更真诚。我没有在网上查他的名字，我觉得让他停留在这本旧书里比较好。
text_pinyin: Zai jiu shudian tao dao yi ben qishi niandai chuban de Qihefu duanpian xiaoshuo ji, yizhe shi wo cong wei tingshuoguo de mingzi. Shu de zhuangtai bu suan hao, fengmian juanjiao, shuji kailie, dan zhenzheng xiyin wo de shi manman de yebian biji. Biji gongzheng er xixiao, yong de shi nage niandai teyou de lanheise gangbi moshui. Biji de neirong rang wo zhami—bu shi putong duzhe de ganxiang, er shi guanyu fanyi benshen de fanfu zhenzhuo. Biru zai yi ge miaoxi huanghun de duanluo pangbian, yizhe xie dao: 'Ci chu yuanwen yong le yi ge baohan guangying jianbain zhi yi de Ewen ci, Zhongwen li meiyou duideng wu. Shi guo 'muse jian chen', tai wenya; 'tian manman hei le', tai baihua. Zuizhong xuan le 'guangxian yi ceng ceng boluo', suiran bu gou zhongshi dan baoliu le yuanwen de dongtai gan.' Zhe zhong fanyi de zhengzha zai mei yi ye shang dou you henji. Geng dadong wo de shi shu mo de yi duan hua: 'Fanyi jiu shi zai liang zhong yuyan zhijian zou gangsi, zuobian shi zhongshi, youbian shi ziran, er ni yongyuan zai shiqu pingheng. Weiyi de anwei shi ou'er mou ge juzi hui ziji zhao dao ta de Zhongwen muyang, na yi ke ni zhi shi chaoxieyuan, bu shi fanyi.' Zhe wei wuming yizhe kongpa yibeizi dou momo wuwen, dan ta zai yebian liuxia de sikao henji, bi hen duo zhuming fanyijia de xuyan dou geng zhencheng. Wo meiyou zai wangshang cha ta de mingzi, wo juede rang ta tingliu zai zhe ben jiu shu li bijiao hao.
text_en: I found a 1970s collection of Chekhov short stories at a used bookstore, translated by a name I'd never heard of. The book wasn't in great shape—cover corners curled, spine cracked—but what really drew me was the margin notes filling every page. The handwriting was neat and tiny, in the blue-black fountain pen ink characteristic of that era. The content fascinated me—not an ordinary reader's reflections, but painstaking deliberations about the translation itself. For instance, beside a passage describing dusk, the translator wrote: 'Here the original uses a Russian word containing the sense of light gradually shifting through shadow. Chinese has no equivalent. Tried 'twilight slowly deepening'—too literary. 'The sky slowly darkened'—too colloquial. Finally chose 'the light peeling away layer by layer'—not faithful enough but preserves the original's dynamic quality.' This translating struggle leaves traces on every page. What moved me more was a passage at the end of the book: 'Translation is walking a tightrope between two languages—faithfulness on the left, naturalness on the right, and you're always losing balance. The only consolation is that occasionally a sentence finds its Chinese form on its own, and in that moment you're merely a copyist, not a translator.' This unknown translator probably spent a lifetime in obscurity, but the thinking traces left in the margins are more sincere than many famous translators' prefaces. I didn't search for his name online. I think it's better to let him remain in this old book.

title: The Weight of Slippers
text_zh: 父亲住院以后，我回家整理他的东西，在门口鞋架上看到他那双棕色皮拖鞋，鞋底已经被磨出了两个浅浅的凹痕——恰好是脚掌和脚跟的位置。我拿起来掂了掂，很轻，但那两个凹痕让它有了某种超出物理重量的分量。母亲说这双拖鞋父亲穿了至少八年，穿坏过一次鞋带，他自己用鱼线缝上了，缝得歪歪扭扭但异常牢固。我低头细看，果然在左脚的鞋带处发现了那段透明的缝线，针脚粗犷得像在缝帆布袋。父亲不是一个惜物的人，衣服随便穿，杯子随便用，唯独这双拖鞋例外。我猜测原因可能很简单：它已经被穿成了脚的形状，换一双新的需要重新磨合，而父亲是那种讨厌一切磨合期的人。但母亲给了另一个解释：「你爸每天下班回来做的第一件事就是换上这双拖鞋。对他来说，穿上拖鞋意味着一天的工作结束了，他终于到家了。」一双拖鞋承载着八年的「到家」，两个凹痕是八年重力的累积。我把它放回了鞋架上，没有收起来。也许等父亲出院回来，他需要在第一时间找到它。
text_pinyin: Fuqin zhuyuan yihou, wo hui jia zhengli ta de dongxi, zai menkou xiejia shang kan dao ta na shuang zongse pi tuoxie, xiedi yijing bei mo chu le liang ge qianqian de aohen—qiahao shi jiaozhang he jiaogen de weizhi. Wo na qilai dian le dian, hen qing, dan na liang ge aohen rang ta you le mou zhong chaochuwuli zhongliang de fenliang. Muqin shuo zhe shuang tuoxie fuqin chuan le zhishao ba nian, chuan huai guo yi ci xiedai, ta ziji yong yuxian feng shang le, feng de waiwainiuniu dan yichang laogu. Wo ditou xi kan, guoran zai zuojiao de xiedai chu faxian le na duan touming de fengxian, zhenjiao cuguang de xiang zai feng fanbu dai. Fuqin bu shi yi ge xiwu de ren, yifu suibian chuan, beizi suibian yong, weidu zhe shuang tuoxie liwai. Wo caice yuanyin keneng hen jiandan: ta yijing bei chuan cheng le jiao de xingzhuang, huan yi shuang xin de xuyao chongxin mohe, er fuqin shi na zhong taoyan yiqie moheqi de ren. Dan muqin gei le ling yi ge jieshi: 'Ni ba mei tian xiaban huilai zuo de di yi jian shi jiu shi huan shang zhe shuang tuoxie. Dui ta lai shuo, chuan shang tuoxie yiwei zhe yi tian de gongzuo jieshu le, ta zhongyu dao jia le.' Yi shuang tuoxie chengzai zhe ba nian de 'dao jia', liang ge aohen shi ba nian zhongli de leiji. Wo ba ta fang hui le xiejia shang, meiyou shou qilai. Yexu deng fuqin chuyuan huilai, ta xuyao zai di yi shijian zhao dao ta.
text_en: After my father was hospitalized, I went home to sort his things and saw his brown leather slippers on the shoe rack by the door, the soles worn into two shallow indentations—right where the ball and heel of the foot rest. I picked them up and weighed them in my hand—very light, but those two indentations gave them a weight beyond physics. My mother said he'd worn these slippers for at least eight years, broke one strap once and sewed it back himself with fishing line—crooked but remarkably sturdy. I looked closely and indeed found the transparent stitching at the left strap, the stitches rough as if sewing a canvas bag. My father isn't a sentimental man about possessions—wears any clothes, uses any cup—except for these slippers. I suspect the reason is simple: they've been worn into the shape of his feet, and switching to a new pair would require breaking in, and my father is the type who detests all break-in periods. But my mother offered another explanation: 'The first thing your dad does coming home from work every day is put on these slippers. For him, putting on slippers means the day's work is over—he's finally home.' A pair of slippers carrying eight years of 'being home,' two indentations the accumulation of eight years of gravity. I put them back on the shoe rack without packing them away. Perhaps when father is discharged and comes home, he'll need to find them right away.

title: The Art of Queueing
text_zh: 在上海生活久了会发现，这座城市的排队文化是一门值得研究的学问。排队在上海不仅仅是等待，它是一种社交行为、一种信息系统，某些时候甚至是一种身份声明。最典型的案例是网红餐厅门口的长队。你会注意到队伍里的人分成几种：真正想吃的、拍照打卡的、以及纯粹被长队吸引过来想看看到底什么东西值得这么多人等的好奇者——第三种人往往排到最后真的会进去消费，因为沉没成本让他们不好意思走开。更有趣的是药店和菜市场的排队。退休阿姨们排队领鸡蛋促销品的时候会自发形成一个信息交换网络：前面的人回报「还剩多少」，后面的人决定是否继续等。整个过程有一种去中心化的效率之美。我认识一位城市社会学研究者，她说：「告诉我一个城市怎么排队，我就能告诉你这个城市的社会信任程度。」上海的排队大体是有序的，但这种有序不是来自规则的约束，而是来自一种精打细算的集体理性——每个人都在计算等待的成本和收益，当收益大于成本，队伍就自然形成了。
text_pinyin: Zai Shanghai shenghuo jiu le hui faxian, zhe zuo chengshi de paidui wenhua shi yi men zhide yanjiu de xuewen. Paidui zai Shanghai bu jinjin shi dengdai, ta shi yi zhong shejiao xingwei, yi zhong xinxi xitong, mou xie shihou shenzhi shi yi zhong shenfen shengming. Zui dianxing de anli shi wanghong canting menkou de changdui. Ni hui zhuyi dao duiwu li de ren fen cheng ji zhong: zhenzheng xiang chi de, paizhao daka de, yiji chuncui bei changdui xiyin guolai xiang kankan daodi shenme dongxi zhide zheme duo ren deng de haoqi zhe—di san zhong ren wangwang pai dao zuihou zhende hui jinqu xiaofei, yinwei chenmochengben rang tamen bu hao yisi zou kai. Geng youqu de shi yaodian he caishichang de paidui. Tuixiu ayi men paidui ling jidan cuxiaopin de shihou hui zifa xingcheng yi ge xinxi jiaohuan wangluo: qianmian de ren huibao 'hai sheng duoshao', houmian de ren jueding shifou jixu deng. Zhenggege guocheng you yi zhong qu zhongxinhua de xiaolv zhi mei. Wo renshi yi wei chengshi shehuixue yanjiuzhe, ta shuo: 'Gaosu wo yi ge chengshi zenme paidui, wo jiu neng gaosu ni zhege chengshi de shehui xinren chengdu.' Shanghai de paidui dati shi youxu de, dan zhe zhong youxu bu shi lai zi guize de yueshu, er shi lai zi yi zhong jingdaxisuan de jiti lixing—mei ge ren dou zai jisuan dengdai de chengben he shouyi, dang shouyi dayu chengben, duiwu jiu ziran xingcheng le.
text_en: Living in Shanghai long enough, you'll find the city's queueing culture is a discipline worth studying. Queueing in Shanghai isn't merely waiting—it's a social behavior, an information system, and sometimes even an identity statement. The most typical example is the long lines outside trendy restaurants. You'll notice people in the queue fall into categories: those who genuinely want to eat, those taking photos for social media check-ins, and pure curiosity-seekers drawn by the long line wanting to see what's worth so many people waiting—this third type often ends up actually going in to consume, because sunk cost makes them embarrassed to leave. Even more interesting is queueing at pharmacies and wet markets. When retired aunties queue for promotional eggs, they spontaneously form an information exchange network: people at the front report 'how many left,' people at the back decide whether to keep waiting. The entire process has a decentralized efficiency beauty. I know an urban sociology researcher who says: 'Tell me how a city queues, and I can tell you that city's level of social trust.' Shanghai's queues are generally orderly, but this order doesn't come from rule enforcement—it comes from a calculating collective rationality. Everyone is computing the cost and benefit of waiting; when benefit exceeds cost, the queue naturally forms.

title: The Night Gardener
text_zh: 住在我楼下的退休物理老师有一个秘密：他只在夜里照料花园。每天凌晨两点左右，他会打着一只微弱的手电筒走进小区公共花坛，蹲下来拔草、松土、修剪枝叶。我是偶然从失眠的窗口发现这件事的。第一次看到的时候还以为是小偷，仔细辨认后才认出他的身影。我后来鼓起勇气问他为什么选择深夜劳作，他的回答出乎意料：「植物在夜里其实更活跃——它们白天光合作用，夜里才真正生长。我老了，觉少，刚好陪它们。而且夜里没人看见，我可以跟它们说话，白天这么做的话邻居们会以为我疯了。」他说他确实会跟每一株植物说话，告诉它们水够不够、根扎得稳不稳。我问他植物能听懂吗，他笑了笑说：「听不懂。但说话的过程让我观察得更仔细。嘴巴在描述的时候，眼睛会注意到平时忽略的细节。」这大概是我听过的关于语言与观察之间关系的最朴素也最精确的解释。他的花园是小区里公认养得最好的，但没有人知道它是在凌晨两点的手电筒光里一棵一棵被照看过来的。
text_pinyin: Zhu zai wo louxia de tuixiu wuli laoshi you yi ge mimi: ta zhi zai yeli zhaoliao huayuan. Mei tian lingchen liang dian zuoyou, ta hui da zhe yi zhi weiruo de shoudiantong zou jin xiaoqu gonggong huatan, dun xialai ba cao, song tu, xiujian zhiye. Wo shi ou'ran cong shimian de chuangkou faxian zhe jian shi de. Di yi ci kan dao de shihou hai yiwei shi xiaotou, zixi bianren hou cai renchu ta de shenying. Wo houlai gu qi yongqi wen ta weishenme xuanze shenye laozuo, ta de huida chuhu yiliao: 'Zhiwu zai yeli qishi geng huoyue—tamen baitian guanghe zuoyong, yeli cai zhenzheng shengzhang. Wo lao le, jue shao, ganghao pei tamen. Erqie yeli mei ren kanjian, wo keyi gen tamen shuo hua, baitian zheme zuo de hua linju men hui yiwei wo feng le.' Ta shuo ta queshi hui gen mei yi zhu zhiwu shuo hua, gaosu tamen shui gou bu gou, gen zha de wen bu wen. Wo wen ta zhiwu neng ting dong ma, ta xiao le xiao shuo: 'Ting bu dong. Dan shuo hua de guocheng rang wo guancha de geng zixi. Zuiba zai miaoshu de shihou, yanjing hui zhuyidao pingshi hulue de xijie.' Zhe dagai shi wo ting guo de guanyu yuyan yu guancha zhijian guanxi de zui pusu ye zui jingque de jieshi. Ta de huayuan shi xiaoqu li gongren yang de zui hao de, dan meiyou ren zhidao ta shi zai lingchen liang dian de shoudiantong guang li yi ke yi ke bei zhaokan guolai de.
text_en: The retired physics teacher living below me has a secret: he only tends the garden at night. Around 2 AM each night, he takes a dim flashlight into the community's public flower beds, squats down to pull weeds, loosen soil, and trim branches. I discovered this by accident from my insomniac window. The first time I thought it was a thief; only after careful looking did I recognize his silhouette. I later mustered the courage to ask why he chose to work at night. His answer was unexpected: 'Plants are actually more active at night—they photosynthesize during the day but truly grow at night. I'm old and sleep little, so I happen to keep them company. Besides, at night no one sees me, so I can talk to them. Doing that in daytime would make the neighbors think I'm crazy.' He said he does speak to every plant, telling them whether they have enough water, whether their roots are firm. I asked if plants can understand. He smiled and said: 'They can't. But the process of speaking makes me observe more carefully. When the mouth is describing, the eyes notice details usually overlooked.' This is perhaps the most plain yet most precise explanation I've heard about the relationship between language and observation. His garden is universally acknowledged as the best-maintained in the community, but nobody knows it was tended plant by plant under a flashlight at 2 AM.

title: The Map Collector
text_zh: 我认识一个从不用导航软件的人。他叫老方，五十多岁，是一个城市规划局的退休干部。他的爱好是收集纸质地图——不是那种旅游纪念品，而是各个年代的城市交通图和区域规划图。他的书房里堆着上百份发黄的旧地图，按年份整齐排列。每次出门前他都会翻出对应区域的纸质地图研究一番，然后靠记忆导航。我问他为什么不用手机，他说：「导航软件给你的是一条最优路线，但它阉割了所有旁枝末节。你只知道从A到B最快怎么走，但不知道C那条巷子曾经是条河，D那个路口的名字来源于一棵已经被砍掉的古樟树。」老方带我在老城区走过一次，那次散步彻底改变了我看城市的方式。他指着每一个不起眼的路名牌告诉我它背后的故事，就像一个考古学家读地层一样读城市的皮肤。他说纸质地图最珍贵的地方在于它记录了「曾经的可能性」——那些被否决的规划方案、被填埋的河流、被改名的街道，在旧地图上都还活着。导航软件只关心「现在怎么走」，而纸质地图保存着「曾经可以怎么走」的记忆。
text_pinyin: Wo renshi yi ge cong bu yong daohang ruanjian de ren. Ta jiao Lao Fang, wushi duo sui, shi yi ge chengshi guihua ju de tuixiu ganbu. Ta de aihao shi shouji zhizhi ditu—bu shi na zhong luyou jinianpin, er shi ge ge niandai de chengshi jiaotong tu he quyu guihua tu. Ta de shufang li dui zhe shang bai fen fahuang de jiu ditu, an nianfen zhengqi pailie. Mei ci chumen qian ta dou hui fan chu duiying quyu de zhizhi ditu yanjiu yi fan, ranhou kao jiyi daohang. Wo wen ta weishenme bu yong shouji, ta shuo: 'Daohang ruanjian gei ni de shi yi tiao zuiyou luxian, dan ta yange le suoyou pangzhi mojie. Ni zhi zhidao cong A dao B zui kuai zenme zou, dan bu zhidao C na tiao xiangzi cengjing shi tiao he, D nage lukou de mingzi laiyuanyu yi ke yijing bei kan diao de gu zhangshu.' Lao Fang dai wo zai lao chengqu zou guo yi ci, na ci sanbu chedi gaibian le wo kan chengshi de fangshi. Ta zhi zhe mei yi ge bu qiyan de luming pai gaosu wo ta beihou de gushi, jiu xiang yi ge kaoguxuejia du diceng yiyang du chengshi de pifu. Ta shuo zhizhi ditu zui zhengui de difang zaiyu ta jilu le 'cengjing de kenengxing'—naxie bei foujue de guihua fang'an, bei tianmai de heliu, bei gaiming de jiedao, zai jiu ditu shang dou hai huo zhe. Daohang ruanjian zhi guanxin 'xianzai zenme zou', er zhizhi ditu baocun zhe 'cengjing keyi zenme zou' de jiyi.
text_en: I know someone who never uses navigation apps. His name is Old Fang, in his fifties, a retired official from the urban planning bureau. His hobby is collecting paper maps—not tourist souvenirs, but city traffic maps and regional planning maps from various eras. His study is stacked with over a hundred yellowed old maps, neatly arranged by year. Before every outing he unfolds the paper map of the relevant area to study, then navigates by memory. I asked why he doesn't use his phone. He said: 'Navigation apps give you the optimal route, but they castrate all the side details. You only know the fastest way from A to B, but don't know that the alley at C used to be a river, or that the intersection at D is named after an ancient camphor tree that's already been cut down.' Old Fang took me on a walk through the old city once, and that stroll completely changed how I see cities. He pointed at every inconspicuous street sign and told me its backstory, reading the city's skin like an archaeologist reads geological strata. He said the most precious thing about paper maps is that they record 'what once could have been'—rejected planning proposals, buried rivers, renamed streets all still live on old maps. Navigation apps only care about 'how to get there now,' while paper maps preserve the memory of 'how one could have once gone.'

title: The Balcony Astronomer
text_zh: 楼上那个独居的年轻人在阳台上架了一台天文望远镜。在城市里看星星这件事本身就有些荒谬——光污染严重到肉眼只能看到最亮的几颗行星和偶尔路过的飞机。但他每个晴朗的夜晚都会准时出现在阳台上，把望远镜对准天空，一待就是几个小时。楼里的人好奇过，有人问他能看到什么，他说：「其实看不到太多。但关键不在于看到什么，而在于那个仰头的动作本身。」他告诉我他是做程序开发的，每天工作十二个小时，对着屏幕上无穷的代码行，视线永远向下或平视。望远镜给了他一个物理上的理由把脖子仰起来，把视线投向无限远。他说这个姿势本身就有治疗效果——当你意识到自己在看的是几万光年外的一颗恒星的残影，白天那些让你焦虑的截止日期和代码冲突就会变得非常小。我后来也试过在阳台上仰望天空，没有望远镜，什么也看不到。但他说的对，重要的是那个仰头的动作。在一个所有人都低头看手机的时代，偶尔仰头的人大概看起来都像异类。可也许正因为异类，那几颗模糊的星才会被看见。
text_pinyin: Lou shang nage duju de nianqing ren zai yangtai shang jia le yi tai tianwen wangyuanjing. Zai chengshi li kan xingxing zhe jian shi benshen jiu youxie huangmiu—guang wuran yanzhong dao rouyan zhi neng kan dao zui liang de ji ke xingxing he ou'er luguo de feiji. Dan ta mei ge qinglang de yewan dou hui zhunshi chuxian zai yangtai shang, ba wangyuanjing dui zhun tiankong, yi dai jiu shi ji ge xiaoshi. Lou li de ren haoqi guo, you ren wen ta neng kan dao shenme, ta shuo: 'Qishi kan bu dao tai duo. Dan guanjian bu zaiyu kan dao shenme, er zaiyu nage yang tou de dongzuo benshen.' Ta gaosu wo ta shi zuo chengxu kaifa de, mei tian gongzuo shi'er ge xiaoshi, dui zhe pingmu shang wuqiong de daima hang, shixian yongyuan xiang xia huo pingshi. Wangyuanjing gei le ta yi ge wuli shang de liyou ba bozi yang qilai, ba shixian tou xiang wuxian yuan. Ta shuo zhege zishi benshen jiu you zhiliao xiaoguo—dang ni yishi dao ziji zai kan de shi ji wan guangnian wai de yi ke hengxing de canying, baitian naxie rang ni jiaolv de jiezhiriqi he daima chongtu jiu hui bian de feichang xiao. Wo houlai ye shi guo zai yangtai shang yangwang tiankong, meiyou wangyuanjing, shenme ye kan bu dao. Dan ta shuo de dui, zhongyao de shi nage yang tou de dongzuo. Zai yi ge suoyou ren dou ditou kan shouji de shidai, ou'er yang tou de ren dagai kan qilai dou xiang yilei. Ke yexu zheng yinwei yilei, na ji ke mohu de xing cai hui bei kanjian.
text_en: The young man living alone upstairs set up a telescope on his balcony. Stargazing in the city is inherently somewhat absurd—light pollution is so severe the naked eye can only see the brightest few planets and the occasional passing airplane. But every clear night he appears punctually on the balcony, points the telescope at the sky, and stays for hours. Building residents have been curious; someone asked what he can see. He said: 'Actually, not much. But the point isn't what you see—it's the act of looking up itself.' He told me he's a software developer, working twelve hours a day staring at infinite lines of code on screen, his gaze always downward or straight ahead. The telescope gives him a physical reason to tilt his neck upward and cast his gaze infinitely far. He said this posture itself has therapeutic effect—when you realize you're looking at the afterimage of a star tens of thousands of light-years away, the deadlines and code conflicts that caused your daytime anxiety become very small. I later tried looking up at the sky from my balcony too, without a telescope, seeing nothing. But he was right—what matters is the act of looking up. In an era where everyone looks down at phones, someone who occasionally looks up probably appears like an oddity. But perhaps precisely because of that oddity, those few blurry stars get seen.

title: The Delivery Rider's Map
text_zh: 跟一位外卖骑手聊了一个下午，彻底颠覆了我对这座城市的认知。他叫小刘，二十七岁，送了三年外卖，每天平均跑四十单。他的大脑里装着一幅跟我们完全不同的城市地图——他不认路名，只认楼盘名；不分东南西北，只分上坡和下坡；不关心风景，只关心哪栋楼的电梯最慢、哪个小区的门禁最难进。他说他能闭着眼睛说出全城三百多栋写字楼的外卖接收点位置，包括绕到后门的哪个台阶上手机信号最好。这种知识是任何地图软件都不会标注的。最让我惊讶的是他对时间的感知方式。他说送外卖以后他的时间观念彻底变了——一切都以分钟为单位思考。红灯等多久、电梯等多久、顾客下来取餐等多久，每一秒都被赋予了具体的经济价值。他算过，一秒钟大约值三分钱。「三年下来，」他说，「我已经不会用正常方式看这座城市了。我看到的不是建筑和街道，而是一张巨大的时间消耗网络。每一个路口、每一部电梯、每一扇门都是一个时间节点。」我觉得他描述的其实是另一种形式的城市考古学——只不过挖掘的不是历史地层，而是当代的时间地层。
text_pinyin: Gen yi wei waimai qishou liao le yi ge xiawu, chedi dianfu le wo dui zhe zuo chengshi de renzhi. Ta jiao Xiao Liu, ershiqi sui, song le san nian waimai, mei tian pingjun pao sishi dan. Ta de danao li zhuang zhe yi fu gen women wanquan butong de chengshi ditu—ta bu ren lu ming, zhi ren loupan ming; bu fen dongnan xibei, zhi fen shangpo he xiapo; bu guanxin fengjing, zhi guanxin na dong lou de dianti zui man, na ge xiaoqu de menjin zui nan jin. Ta shuo ta neng bi zhe yanjing shuo chu quancheng sanbai duo dong xiezilou de waimai jieshou dian weizhi, baokuo rao dao houmen de nage taijie shang shouji xinhao zui hao. Zhe zhong zhishi shi renhe ditu ruanjian dou bu hui biaozhude. Zui rang wo jingya de shi ta dui shijian de ganzhi fangshi. Ta shuo song waimai yihou ta de shijian guannian chedi bian le—yiqie dou yi fenzhong wei danwei sikao. Hongdeng deng duo jiu, dianti deng duo jiu, guke xialai qu can deng duo jiu, mei yi miao dou bei fuyu le juti de jingji jiazhi. Ta suan guo, yi miaozhoong dayue zhi san fen qian. 'San nian xialai,' ta shuo, 'wo yijing bu hui yong zhengchang fangshi kan zhe zuo chengshi le. Wo kan dao de bu shi jianzhu he jiedao, er shi yi zhang juda de shijian xiaohao wangluo. Mei yi ge lukou, mei yi bu dianti, mei yi shan men dou shi yi ge shijian jiedian.' Wo juede ta miaoshu de qishi shi ling yi zhong xingshi de chengshi kaoguxue—zhibuguo wajue de bu shi lishi diceng, er shi dangdai de shijian diceng.
text_en: After chatting with a delivery rider for an entire afternoon, my understanding of this city was completely overturned. His name is Xiao Liu, twenty-seven, three years delivering food, averaging forty orders daily. His brain contains a city map entirely different from ours—he doesn't know street names, only development names; doesn't distinguish east-south-west-north, only uphill and downhill; doesn't care about scenery, only about which building's elevator is slowest, which community's gate access is hardest to get through. He says he can recite with eyes closed the food delivery pickup locations for over three hundred office buildings citywide, including which back-door staircase has the best phone signal. This is knowledge no map software would ever mark. What amazed me most was his perception of time. He said after becoming a delivery rider his sense of time completely changed—everything is thought of in minutes. How long to wait at a red light, how long to wait for an elevator, how long for the customer to come down to get the food—every second is assigned a concrete economic value. He calculated that one second is worth about three fen. 'After three years,' he said, 'I can no longer see this city the normal way. What I see isn't buildings and streets, but a giant time-consumption network. Every intersection, every elevator, every door is a time node.' I think what he described is actually another form of urban archaeology—only instead of excavating historical strata, it's excavating contemporary time strata.

title: The Sound of Construction
text_zh: 连续三个月，每天早上七点半准时被对面工地的打桩声震醒。那种声音不是简单的噪音——它有节奏，沉闷而规律，像一颗巨大的心脏在地底跳动。起初我跟所有邻居一样愤怒，打过投诉电话，在业主群里发过牢骚。但三个月以后，一种奇怪的适应发生了：我开始依赖那个声音来确认时间。闹钟可以被按掉，但打桩声不行，它比任何人造的提醒都更忠实。更奇怪的是，我开始从中辨认出细微的变化——周一的第一锤总是格外有力，大概是工人们周末休息后蓄积了能量；周五下午的节奏明显加快，赶工的急切透过混凝土传到了我的窗户。有一天打桩声突然停了，工地进入了下一个施工阶段。那天早上我反而失眠般地醒得很早，房间里安静得让人不适应。我站在窗边看着对面工地上立起来的钢筋框架，意识到一件事：那些让我烦躁了三个月的声音，其实一直在用最诚实的方式报告着一栋建筑从无到有的过程。我曾经是它最不情愿的听众，但也因此成了它最忠实的见证者。
text_pinyin: Lianxu san ge yue, mei tian zaoshang qi dian ban zhunshi bei duimian gongdi de dazhuang sheng zhen xing. Na zhong shengyin bu shi jiandan de zaoyin—ta you jiezou, chenmen er guilu, xiang yi ke juda de xinzang zai didi tiaodong. Qichu wo gen suoyou linju yiyang fennu, da guo tousu dianhua, zai yezhu qun li fa guo laosao. Dan san ge yue yihou, yi zhong qiguai de shiying fasheng le: wo kaishi yilai nage shengyin lai queren shijian. Naozhong keyi bei an diao, dan dazhuang sheng bu xing, ta bi renhe renzao de tixing dou geng zhongshi. Geng qiguai de shi, wo kaishi cong zhong bianren chu xiwei de bianhua—zhouyi de di yi chui zong shi gewai youli, dagai shi gongren men zhoumo xiuxi hou xuji le nengliang; zhouwu xiawu de jiezou mingxian jiakuai, gangong de jiqie touguo hunningtu chuan dao le wo de chuanghu. You yi tian dazhuang sheng turan ting le, gongdi jinru le xia yi ge shigong jieduan. Na tian zaoshang wo fan'er shimian ban de xing de hen zao, fangjian li anjing de rang ren bu shiying. Wo zhan zai chuang bian kan zhe duimian gongdi shang li qilai de gangjin kuangjia, yishi dao yi jian shi: naxie rang wo fanzao le san ge yue de shengyin, qishi yizhi zai yong zui chengshi de fangshi baogao zhe yi dong jianzhu cong wu dao you de guocheng. Wo cengjing shi ta zui bu qingyuan de tingzhong, dan ye yinci cheng le ta zui zhongshi de jianzheng zhe.
text_en: For three consecutive months, every morning at exactly 7:30 I was shaken awake by the pile-driving sounds from the construction site across the street. That sound wasn't simple noise—it had rhythm, muffled and regular, like a giant heart beating underground. At first, like all my neighbors, I was furious—made complaint calls, vented in the homeowners' group chat. But after three months, a strange adaptation occurred: I began relying on that sound to confirm the time. An alarm can be snoozed, but pile-driving cannot; it's more faithful than any man-made reminder. Stranger still, I began discerning subtle variations—Monday's first blow was always notably forceful, presumably from energy the workers stored over the weekend; Friday afternoon's rhythm noticeably quickened, the urgency of rushing to finish transmitting through concrete to my window. One day the pile-driving suddenly stopped; the site had entered its next construction phase. That morning I woke early as if from insomnia, the room uncomfortably quiet. I stood by the window looking at the steel framework rising on the site opposite and realized something: those sounds that annoyed me for three months had actually been reporting, in the most honest way, the process of a building coming into existence from nothing. I was once its most reluctant audience, but thereby became its most faithful witness.

title: The Underground Bookstore
text_zh: 城市里还存活着的独立书店大多学会了伪装。有些变成了咖啡馆，卖咖啡顺带卖书；有些变成了文创空间，书只是背景墙的装饰。但我知道一家完全不伪装的书店，它在一栋老居民楼的半地下室里，没有招牌，没有公众号，你必须知道门牌号才能找到。老板姓严，四十多岁，以前在出版社做编辑，辞职后开了这家店。店里只卖他自己读过的书，每一本都附有他手写的简短书评，夹在扉页里。我问他为什么不做线上销售，他摇摇头说：「卖书不是卖商品，是把一个人读过的东西递给另一个人。这个'递'的动作必须是面对面的，我要看到对方的脸，判断这本书是否适合他。」他确实会拒绝卖书给某些人——不是势利，而是他真的觉得那本书跟那个人不匹配。有一次我想买一本存在主义哲学的书，他看了我一眼说：「你现在的状态不适合读这个，先拿这本散文集去吧。」我当时有点不高兴，但一个月后回想才觉得他说得对。半地下室的书店潮湿昏暗，空间狭小，但每一次走进去都像进入一个有人把关的私人图书馆。在算法推荐统治一切的时代，一个凭直觉帮你选书的真人，也许是最后的奢侈品。
text_pinyin: Chengshi li hai cunhuo zhe de duli shudian daduo xue hui le weizhuang. Youxie bian cheng le kafei guan, mai kafei shundai mai shu; youxie bian cheng le wenchuang kongjian, shu zhi shi beijing qiang de zhuangshi. Dan wo zhidao yi jia wanquan bu weizhuang de shudian, ta zai yi dong lao jumin lou de ban dixiashi li, meiyou zhaopai, meiyou gongzhonghao, ni bixu zhidao menpai hao cai neng zhao dao. Laoban xing Yan, sishi duo sui, yiqian zai chubanshe zuo bianji, cizhi hou kai le zhe jia dian. Dian li zhi mai ta ziji du guo de shu, mei yi ben dou fu you ta shouxie de jianduan shuping, jia zai feiyelili. Wo wen ta weishenme bu zuo xianshang xiaoshou, ta yaoyao tou shuo: 'Mai shu bu shi mai shangpin, shi ba yi ge ren du guo de dongxi di gei ling yi ge ren. Zhege di de dongzuo bixu shi mianduimian de, wo yao kan dao duifang de lian, panduan zhe ben shu shifou shihe ta.' Ta queshi hui jujue mai shu gei mou xie ren—bu shi shili, er shi ta zhende juede na ben shu gen na ge ren bu pipei. You yi ci wo xiang mai yi ben cunzai zhuyi zhexue de shu, ta kan le wo yi yan shuo: 'Ni xianzai de zhuangtai bu shihe du zhege, xian na zhe ben sanwen ji qu ba.' Wo dangshi you dian bu gaoxing, dan yi ge yue hou huixiang cai juede ta shuo de dui. Ban dixiashi de shudian chaoshi hun'an, kongjian xiaxiao, dan mei yi ci zou jinqu dou xiang jinru yi ge you ren baguan de siren tushuguan. Zai suanfa tuijian tongzhi yiqie de shidai, yi ge ping zhijue bang ni xuan shu de zhenren, yexu shi zuihou de shechipin.
text_en: Most surviving independent bookstores in the city have learned to disguise themselves. Some became cafes that sell coffee and incidentally books; others became cultural creative spaces where books are mere wall decoration. But I know a bookstore that doesn't disguise itself at all—it's in the semi-basement of an old residential building, with no sign, no social media account; you must know the door number to find it. The owner, surnamed Yan, is in his forties, formerly an editor at a publishing house who quit to open this shop. He only sells books he's personally read, each accompanied by his handwritten brief review tucked into the title page. I asked why he doesn't sell online. He shook his head: 'Selling books isn't selling commodities—it's handing something one person has read to another. This act of 'handing over' must be face to face; I need to see the other person's face and judge whether the book suits them.' He does refuse to sell books to certain people—not snobbery, but he genuinely feels the book doesn't match the person. Once I wanted to buy an existentialist philosophy book; he glanced at me and said: 'Your current state isn't right for this. Take this essay collection first.' I was slightly annoyed at the time, but a month later realized he was right. The semi-basement bookstore is damp, dim, and cramped, but every time you walk in it's like entering a private library with someone standing guard. In an era where algorithmic recommendations rule everything, a real person who selects books for you by intuition may be the last luxury.

title: The Library's Secret Hours
text_zh: 在区图书馆工作的朋友告诉我一个有趣的规律：图书馆最安静的时间不是开馆时，而是下午两点到三点之间——这是午饭后的倦怠期，大多数人在这个时段要么打盹要么走神。但这恰恰是图书馆最「忠实」的读者群体出没的时间。他们是一群退休老人，每天准时出现，各自占据固定的座位，翻阅固定的报纸和杂志。座位的归属已经形成了一种默契的领地制度——没有明文规定，但如果有人坐了「别人的」位置，空气里会弥漫一种微妙的不快。最让我感兴趣的是图书馆的另一个秘密时段：周二和周四的早上九点，总有几位母亲带着学龄前的孩子来听故事会。图书馆管理员小陈说，这些母亲其实不全是为了孩子——她们需要一个正当的理由出门，需要跟其他成年人说说话，需要一个不用解释自己为什么不上班的公共空间。「图书馆的功能远比'借书'复杂得多，」小陈说，「它其实是一个伪装成文化机构的社区庇护所。」
text_pinyin: Zai qu tushuguan gongzuo de pengyou gaosu wo yi ge youqu de guilu: tushuguan zui anjing de shijian bu shi kai guan shi, er shi xiawu liang dian dao san dian zhijian—zhe shi wufan hou de juandai qi, daduoshu ren zai zhege shiduan yaome dadun yaome zoushen. Dan zhe qiaqia shi tushuguan zui 'zhongshi' de duzhe qunti chumo de shijian. Tamen shi yi qun tuixiu laoren, mei tian zhunshi chuxian, gezi zhanju guding de zuowei, fanyue guding de baozhi he zazhi. Zuowei de guishu yijing xingcheng le yi zhong moqi de lingdi zhidu—meiyou mingwen guiding, dan ruguo you ren zuo le 'bieren de' weizhi, kongqi li hui miman yi zhong weimiao de bukuai. Zui rang wo gan xingqu de shi tushuguan de ling yi ge mimi shiduan: zhou'er he zhousi de zaoshang jiu dian, zong you ji wei muqin dai zhe xuelingqian de haizi lai ting gushihui. Tushuguan guanliyuan Xiao Chen shuo, zhexie muqin qishi bu quan shi weile haizi—tamen xuyao yi ge zhengdang de liyou chumen, xuyao gen qita chengnianren shuo shuo hua, xuyao yi ge bu yong jieshi ziji weishenme bu shangban de gonggong kongjian. 'Tushuguan de gongneng yuan bi jie shu fuza de duo,' Xiao Chen shuo, 'ta qishi shi yi ge weizhuang cheng wenhua jigou de shequ bihuosuo.'
text_en: A friend who works at the district library told me an interesting pattern: the library's quietest time isn't opening hour but between 2 and 3 PM—the post-lunch drowsy period when most people are either dozing or zoning out. But this is precisely when the library's most 'loyal' reader group appears. They're a group of retirees who arrive punctually each day, each claiming a fixed seat, reading fixed newspapers and magazines. Seat ownership has formed a tacit territorial system—no written rules, but if someone sits in 'another's' spot, a subtle displeasure permeates the air. What interests me most is the library's other secret time slot: Tuesday and Thursday mornings at nine, a few mothers always bring preschool children for story time. Librarian Xiao Chen says these mothers aren't entirely there for the children—they need a legitimate reason to leave the house, need to talk to other adults, need a public space where they don't have to explain why they're not at work. 'The library's function is far more complex than borrowing books,' Xiao Chen says. 'It's actually a community shelter disguised as a cultural institution.'

title: The Night Shift Nurse
text_zh: 在医院陪夜的时候，凌晨三点的走廊像另一个世界。日光灯只开了一半，墙上的影子拉得很长，偶尔有输液架的轮子在地板上滑过，发出细小而尖锐的声音。夜班护士小赵每隔两小时巡房一次，她穿着白大褂走过长长的走廊时脚步极轻，连鞋底摩擦地板的声音都被刻意压到最低。她告诉我她学了三年才掌握这种走路方式——「在病房里，声音是有重量的。白天人多，噪音被稀释了，但凌晨三点任何一个细微的声响都会被放大十倍。一个关门声可能让刚睡着的病人惊醒，多醒一次就少恢复一点。」她随身带着一个小笔记本，记录着每个病人的睡眠规律——谁容易惊醒，谁会梦游，谁需要在固定时间翻身防止褥疮。这些信息不在病历里，却决定着病人夜间的生活质量。我问她做夜班最大的挑战是什么，她想了想说：「不是困，是寂寞。凌晨的医院只有你是清醒的，你要对着一整层楼的沉睡负责，这种责任感有时候重得让人喘不过气。」天快亮的时候，她站在走廊尽头的窗前看了一会儿渐渐发白的天空，然后回过头对我说：「每次看到天亮我都会松一口气——又安全地陪他们度过了一个夜晚。」
text_pinyin: Zai yiyuan pei ye de shihou, lingchen san dian de zoulang xiang ling yi ge shijie. Riguangdeng zhi kai le yiban, qiang shang de yingzi la de hen chang, ou'er you shuyejia de lunzi zai diban shang hua guo, fachu xixiao er jianirrui de shengyin. Yeban hushi Xiao Zhao mei ge liang xiaoshi xunfang yi ci, ta chuan zhe bai dagua zou guo changchang de zoulang shi jiaobu ji qing, lian xiedi moca diban de shengyin dou bei keyi ya dao zuidi. Ta gaosu wo ta xue le san nian cai zhangwo zhe zhong zoulu fangshi—'Zai bingfang li, shengyin shi you zhongliang de. Baitian ren duo, zaoyin bei xishi le, dan lingchen san dian renhe yi ge xiwei de shengxiang dou hui bei fangda shi bei. Yi ge guan men sheng keneng rang gang shui zhao de bingren jingxing, duo xing yi ci jiu shao huifu yi dian.' Ta suishen dai zhe yi ge xiao bijiben, jilu zhe mei ge bingren de shuimian guilu—shei rongyi jingxing, shei hui mengyou, shei xuyao zai guding shijian fanshen fangzhi ruchuang. Zhexie xinxi bu zai bingli li, que jueding zhe bingren yeqian de shenghuo zhiliang. Wo wen ta zuo yeban zuida de tiaozhan shi shenme, ta xiang le xiang shuo: 'Bu shi kun, shi jimo. Lingchen de yiyuan zhi you ni shi qingxing de, ni yao dui zhe yi zheng ceng lou de chenshui fuze, zhe zhong zerenggan youshihou zhong de rang ren chuan bu guo qi.' Tian kuai liang de shihou, ta zhan zai zoulang jintou de chuang qian kan le yihuir jianjian fabai de tiankong, ranhou hui guo tou dui wo shuo: 'Mei ci kan dao tian liang wo dou hui song yi kou qi—you anquan de pei tamen duguo le yi ge yewan.'
text_en: During my night vigil at the hospital, the corridor at 3 AM was like another world. Only half the fluorescent lights were on, shadows on the walls stretched long, occasionally an IV stand's wheels slid across the floor with a small sharp sound. Night nurse Xiao Zhao makes rounds every two hours; her footsteps as she walks down the long corridor in her white coat are extremely light, even the sound of shoe soles on the floor deliberately suppressed. She told me it took three years to master this way of walking—'In hospital wards, sound has weight. Daytime noise is diluted by crowds, but at 3 AM any tiny sound is amplified tenfold. One door closing might wake a patient who just fell asleep, and one extra awakening means a bit less recovery.' She carries a small notebook recording each patient's sleep patterns—who startles easily, who sleepwalks, who needs to be turned at fixed times to prevent bedsores. This information isn't in medical charts but determines patients' nighttime quality of life. I asked her what the biggest challenge of night shift is. She thought a moment: 'Not sleepiness—loneliness. In the predawn hospital you're the only one awake, responsible for an entire floor of sleepers. That weight of responsibility sometimes makes it hard to breathe.' As dawn approached, she stood at the corridor's end window watching the sky gradually lighten, then turned to me and said: 'Every time I see daylight I breathe a sigh of relief—I've safely accompanied them through another night.'

title: The Midnight Caller
text_zh: 连续三个月，每周三午夜十二点零五分，我的座机电话都会响。拿起听筒，那头沉默几秒，然后挂断。来电显示是一个本地号码，但回拨过去永远是忙音。我一开始以为是骚扰电话或者技术故障，但它的精确度让人起疑——每周三，不多不少，十二点零五分，持续响铃五声后如果我没接就自动挂断；如果我接了，对方沉默约三秒再挂。朋友建议我报警，但这算什么案件呢？对方既没说话也没威胁。我试过在周三之前拔掉电话线，那天夜里果然没有来电，但下一个周三恢复后照常响。我甚至换了一个新电话号码，但第三个周三，新号码照样在十二点零五分响了。这让我后背发凉，因为我确信没有人知道我的新号码。最终我选择了一种可能是最不理性的应对方式：每到周三午夜，我提前把茶泡好，坐在电话旁边等着它响，然后接起来，在对方沉默的三秒钟里说一句「晚安」再挂掉。第四个月的时候，电话突然不再响了。我至今不知道那三个月的来电意味着什么，但我偶尔会想起那些深夜的「晚安」——也许这个世界上，有些事不需要解释，只需要有人回应。
text_pinyin: Lianxu san ge yue, mei zhousan wuye shi'er dian ling wu fen, wo de zuoji dianhua dou hui xiang. Na qi tingtong, natou chenmo ji miao, ranhou guaduan. Laidian xianshi shi yi ge bendi haoma, dan hui bo guoqu yongyuan shi mangyin. Wo yi kaishi yiwei shi saorao dianhua huozhe jishu guzhang, dan ta de jingqudu rang ren qi yi—mei zhousan, bu duo bu shao, shi'er dian ling wu fen, chixu xiangle wu sheng hou ruguo wo mei jie jiu zidong guaduan; ruguo wo jie le, duifang chenmo yue san miao zai gua. Pengyou jianyi wo baojing, dan zhe suan shenme anjian ne? Duifang ji mei shuo hua ye mei weixie. Wo shi guo zai zhousan zhiqian ba diao dianhua xian, na tian yeli guoran meiyou laidian, dan xia yi ge zhousan huifu hou zhao chang xiang. Wo shenzhi huan le yi ge xin dianhua haoma, dan di san ge zhousan, xin haoma zhaoyang zai shi'er dian ling wu fen xiang le. Zhe rang wo houbei fa liang, yinwei wo quexin meiyou ren zhidao wo de xin haoma. Zuizhong wo xuanze le yi zhong keneng shi zui bu lixing de yingdui fangshi: mei dao zhousan wuye, wo tiqian ba cha pao hao, zuo zai dianhua pangbian deng zhe ta xiang, ranhou jie qilai, zai duifang chenmo de san miao zhong li shuo yi ju 'wan'an' zai gua diao. Di si ge yue de shihou, dianhua turan bu zai xiang le. Wo zhijin bu zhidao na san ge yue de laidian yiwei zhe shenme, dan wo ou'er hui xiangqi naxie shenye de 'wan'an'—yexu zhege shijie shang, youxie shi bu xuyao jieshi, zhi xuyao you ren huiying.
text_en: For three consecutive months, every Wednesday at exactly 12:05 AM, my landline phone would ring. I'd pick up the receiver; the other end would go silent for a few seconds, then hang up. Caller ID showed a local number, but calling back always got a busy signal. At first I thought it was a spam call or technical glitch, but its precision was suspicious—every Wednesday, neither more nor less, at 12:05, ringing five times before auto-disconnecting if unanswered; if I answered, the other end was silent about three seconds before hanging up. Friends suggested I call the police, but what kind of case would this be? The caller neither spoke nor threatened. I tried unplugging the phone line before Wednesday; indeed no call came that night, but the next Wednesday after reconnecting it rang as usual. I even changed to a new phone number, but the third Wednesday, the new number rang at 12:05 just the same. This sent chills down my spine, because I was certain no one knew my new number. Ultimately I chose what is probably the least rational response: every Wednesday midnight I'd brew tea in advance, sit by the phone waiting for it to ring, then pick up and say 'good night' during the caller's three seconds of silence before hanging up. In the fourth month, the phone suddenly stopped ringing. I still don't know what those three months of calls meant, but I occasionally think of those late-night 'good nights'—perhaps in this world, some things don't need explanation, only someone to respond.

title: The Garden That Blooms at Night
text_zh: 隔壁别墅的花园有一个违反常识的现象：某些花只在夜间开放。我说的不是昙花或夜来香这些本来就是夜开的品种，而是明明应该白天开的月季、茉莉和栀子花，到了那个花园里却反过来了——白天闭合，入夜后才渐渐绽放。园艺师朋友来看过一次，百思不得其解。他检查了土壤酸碱度、光照时间、浇水频率，所有参数都在正常范围内。唯一异常的是花园的光照环境：隔壁那栋别墅的主人是个夜猫子，每天从傍晚开始亮灯，客厅的落地窗正对花园，灯光一直亮到凌晨三四点。园艺师推测，长年累月的人工光源可能扰乱了植物的昼夜节律，让它们「误以为」夜间才是白天。如果这个解释成立，那么这个花园里上演的是一种微型的生态错位——人类的生活节奏强行改写了植物的生物钟。我站在围墙外面看过几次那个花园的夜间花期，月光下绽放的茉莉确实有一种诡异的美，像一群在错误时区醒来的旅人，茫然却灿烂。也许美有时候恰恰产生于错位——在不该开的时候开了，在不该亮的时候亮了，反而比一切按部就班更令人注目。
text_pinyin: Gebi bieshu de huayuan you yi ge weifan changshi de xianxiang: mou xie hua zhi zai yeqian kaifang. Wo shuo de bu shi tanhua huo yelaixin zhexie benlai jiu shi ye kai de pinzhong, er shi mingming yinggai baitian kai de yueji, moli he zhizihua, dao le nage huayuan li que fan guolai le—baitian bihe, ru ye hou cai jianjian zhanfang. Yuanyi shi pengyou lai kan guo yi ci, baisibujieq. Ta jiancha le turang suanjian du, guangzhao shijian, jiao shui pinlv, suoyou canshu dou zai zhengchang fanwei nei. Weiyi yichang de shi huayuan de guangzhao huanjing: gebi na dong bieshu de zhuren shi ge yemao zi, mei tian cong bangwan kaishi liang deng, keting de luodi chuang zheng dui huayuan, dengguang yizhi liang dao lingchen san si dian. Yuanyi shi tuice, changnianleiyue de rengong guangyuan keneng raoluan le zhiwu de zhouye jielv, rang tamen 'wu yiwei' yeqian cai shi baitian. Ruguo zhege jieshi chengli, name zhege huayuan li shangyan de shi yi zhong weixing de shengtai cuowei—renlei de shenghuo jiezou qiangxing gaixie le zhiwu de shengwuzhong. Wo zhan zai weiqiang waimian kan guo ji ci nage huayuan de yeqian huaqi, yueguang xia zhanfang de moli queshi you yi zhong guiyi de mei, xiang yi qun zai cuowu shiqu xing lai de lvren, mangran que canlan. Yexu mei youshihou qiaqia chansheng yu cuowei—zai bu gai kai de shihou kai le, zai bu gai liang de shihou liang le, fan'er bi yiqie anbu jiuban geng ling ren zhumu.
text_en: The garden of the villa next door has a phenomenon that defies common sense: certain flowers only bloom at night. I don't mean night-blooming species like moonflowers or tuberoses, but roses, jasmine, and gardenias that should bloom by day—in that garden they're reversed, closing during daytime and gradually opening after dark. A horticulturist friend came to look once and was completely baffled. He checked soil pH, light exposure, watering frequency—all parameters within normal range. The only anomaly was the garden's light environment: the villa's owner is a night owl who turns on lights starting at dusk, the living room's floor-to-ceiling windows facing the garden, lights staying on until 3 or 4 AM. The horticulturist speculated that years of artificial light may have disrupted the plants' circadian rhythms, making them 'believe' nighttime is daytime. If this explanation holds, the garden stages a miniature ecological displacement—human living rhythms forcibly rewriting plants' biological clocks. I've stood outside the wall watching the garden's nighttime bloom several times. Jasmine opening under moonlight does have an eerie beauty, like a group of travelers who woke up in the wrong time zone—bewildered yet radiant. Perhaps beauty sometimes arises precisely from displacement—blooming when it shouldn't, shining when it shouldn't, more arresting than everything proceeding on schedule.

title: The Handwriting I Lost
text_zh: 前几天需要手写一份材料，拿起笔的时候发现手在微微发抖——不是紧张，而是这个动作太久没做了。我已经记不清上一次长时间用笔写字是什么时候，也许是五年前签房屋合同，也许更早。写出来的字歪歪扭扭，跟我记忆中自己的笔迹完全不同。小学时候练过好几年书法的手，如今连楷书都写不端正了。这让我产生了一种奇特的丧失感。我翻出大学时的笔记本对比，发现二十岁时我的字清秀而紧凑，每一笔都有一种年轻人特有的用力感，仿佛要把墨水按进纸里。现在的字松散、犹豫、缺乏信心，像一个人站在太久没走过的路上不知道往哪儿迈步。字迹是比照片更诚实的自画像——它记录的不是你长什么样，而是你写字那一刻手的状态、心的状态、甚至呼吸的状态。我们这一代人大概是最后一批有「笔迹记忆」的人了。等手写完全被键盘取代以后，一种极其私密的身份标识就永远消失了。未来的人也许很难理解，为什么我们会因为看到一行旧笔迹而感到亲切——因为他们从未用手握住过一支笔，感受过墨水在纸上留下的轻微阻力。
text_pinyin: Qian ji tian xuyao shouxie yi fen cailiao, na qi bi de shihou faxian shou zai weiwei fadou—bu shi jinzhang, er shi zhege dongzuo tai jiu mei zuo le. Wo yijing ji bu qing shang yi ci chang shijian yong bi xiezi shi shenme shihou, yexu shi wu nian qian qian fangwu hetong, yexu geng zao. Xie chulai de zi waiwainiuniu, gen wo jiyi zhong ziji de biji wanquan butong. Xiaoxue shihou lian guo hao ji nian shufa de shou, rujin lian kaishu dou xie bu duanzheng le. Zhe rang wo chansheng le yi zhong qite de sangshi gan. Wo fan chu daxue shi de bijiben duibi, faxian ershi sui shi wo de zi qingxiu er jincu, mei yi bi dou you yi zhong nianqing ren teyou de yongli gan, fangfu yao ba moshui an jin zhi li. Xianzai de zi songsan, youyu, quefa xinxin, xiang yi ge ren zhan zai tai jiu mei zou guo de lu shang bu zhidao wang nar mai bu. Ziji shi bi zhaopian geng chengshi de zihuaxiang—ta jilu de bu shi ni zhang shenme yang, er shi ni xiezi na yi ke shou de zhuangtai, xin de zhuangtai, shenzhi huxi de zhuangtai. Women zhe yi dai ren dagai shi zuihou yi pi you 'biji jiyi' de ren le. Deng shouxie wanquan bei jianpan qudai yihou, yi zhong jiqi simi de shenfen biaoshi jiu yongyuan xiaoshi le. Weilai de ren yexu hen nan lijie, weishenme women hui yinwei kan dao yi hang jiu biji er gandao qinqie—yinwei tamen cong wei yong shou wo zhu guo yi zhi bi, ganshouguo moshui zai zhi shang liuxia de qingwei zuli.
text_en: A few days ago I needed to handwrite a document. When I picked up the pen, I noticed my hand was trembling slightly—not from nervousness, but because the action hadn't been performed in so long. I can't remember the last time I wrote by hand for an extended period—maybe signing a housing contract five years ago, maybe earlier. The characters came out crooked and nothing like the handwriting I remembered. The hand that practiced calligraphy for several years in elementary school now can't even write regular script properly. This gave me a peculiar sense of loss. I dug out my college notebooks to compare and found that at twenty my characters were elegant and compact, each stroke carrying a young person's particular forcefulness, as if pressing ink into the paper. My current writing is loose, hesitant, lacking confidence—like someone standing on a path they haven't walked in too long, unsure where to step. Handwriting is a more honest self-portrait than photographs—it records not what you look like, but the state of your hand, your mind, even your breathing at the moment of writing. Our generation is probably the last to have 'handwriting memory.' Once handwriting is completely replaced by keyboards, an extremely private identity marker will disappear forever. Future people may find it hard to understand why we feel warmth seeing a line of old handwriting—because they will never have held a pen, feeling the slight resistance of ink on paper.

title: The Photograph I Don't Recognize
text_zh: 整理旧手机的时候，翻到一张六年前在西藏拍的照片——照片里是我自己，站在纳木错湖边，笑得非常灿烂。问题是我完全不记得拍这张照片的场景。不是健忘，而是照片里那个人的笑容、站姿、甚至穿的衣服都让我感到陌生，仿佛在看一个长得跟我很像的陌生人。我试图回忆那次旅行的细节，只能想起一些模糊的片段——高原反应的头痛、酥油茶的气味、夜里帐篷外的风声。但那个灿烂的笑容背后是什么情绪，我完全想不起来。这让我意识到一个不舒服的事实：六年前的那个我和现在的我，也许在生物学上是同一个人，但在心理学上已经是两个不同的人了。细胞更换了好几轮，经历重塑了性格，连对快乐的定义都变了——六年前的我大概觉得站在壮丽的湖边就是快乐，而现在的我更可能在一杯安静的咖啡里找到类似的感觉。照片是时间的陷阱：它保存了一个瞬间的你，但那个瞬间的你已经不存在了，存在的只是关于他的一堆像素。我把那张照片存进了一个单独的文件夹，取名叫「前任」——不是别人，是以前的自己。
text_pinyin: Zhengli jiu shouji de shihou, fan dao yi zhang liu nian qian zai Xizang pai de zhaopian—zhaopian li shi wo ziji, zhan zai Namucuo hu bian, xiao de feichang canlan. Wenti shi wo wanquan bu jide pai zhe zhang zhaopian de changjing. Bu shi jianwang, er shi zhaopian li nage ren de xiaorong, zhanzhi, shenzhi chuan de yifu dou rang wo gandao mosheng, fangfu zai kan yi ge zhang de gen wo hen xiang de moshengren. Wo shitu huiyi na ci lxing de xijie, zhi neng xiangqi yixie mohu de pianduan—gaoyuan fanying de toutong, suyoucha de qiwei, yeli zhangpeng wai de fengsheng. Dan nage canlan de xiaorong beihou shi shenme qingxu, wo wanquan xiang bu qilai. Zhe rang wo yishi dao yi ge bu shufu de shishi: liu nian qian de nage wo he xianzai de wo, yexu zai shengwuxue shang shi tong yi ge ren, dan zai xinlixue shang yijing shi liang ge butong de ren le. Xibao genghuan le hao ji lun, jingli chongsu le xingge, lian dui kuaile de dingyi dou bian le—liu nian qian de wo dagai juede zhan zai zhuangli de hu bian jiu shi kuaile, er xianzai de wo geng keneng zai yi bei anjing de kafei li zhao dao leisi de ganjue. Zhaopian shi shijian de xianjing: ta baocun le yi ge shunjian de ni, dan nage shunjian de ni yijing bu cunzai le, cunzai de zhi shi guanyu ta de yi dui xiangsu. Wo ba na zhang zhaopian cun jin le yi ge dandude wenjian jia, qu ming jiao 'qianren'—bu shi bieren, shi yiqian de ziji.
text_en: Sorting through an old phone, I found a photo from six years ago taken in Tibet—it's me, standing by Namtso Lake, smiling radiantly. The problem is I have absolutely no memory of the scene when it was taken. Not forgetfulness—the smile, posture, even the clothes of the person in the photo feel foreign to me, as if looking at a stranger who closely resembles me. I tried recalling details of that trip but could only summon vague fragments—altitude sickness headache, the smell of butter tea, wind outside the tent at night. But what emotion was behind that radiant smile, I cannot recall at all. This made me realize an uncomfortable truth: the me from six years ago and the current me may be biologically the same person, but psychologically we're already two different people. Cells have been replaced several times over, experiences have reshaped personality, even the definition of happiness has changed—six years ago I probably felt that standing beside a magnificent lake was happiness, while the current me is more likely to find a similar feeling in a quiet cup of coffee. Photographs are time traps: they preserve an instantaneous you, but that instantaneous you no longer exists—what remains is merely a pile of pixels about them. I saved the photo in a separate folder named 'Ex'—not someone else, but my former self.

title: The Group Chat Etiquette
text_zh: 我目前同时存在于六十三个微信群里，其中大约五十个处于永久静音状态。每个群都有自己的潜规则，违反任何一条都可能引发社交灾难。比如家族群：只能发鼓励性质的内容和养生文章，绝对不能发政治观点或任何可能引发争论的话题，否则你大姨会在下次家庭聚餐时意味深长地看你一眼。同学群的规则更微妙：可以回忆往事但不能提当年谁追过谁，可以炫耀孩子但程度不能超过组委会默认的阈值——一般来说，考上重点中学可以发，考上清华就只能低调私聊了。最复杂的是工作群。老板发了一条消息以后，第一个回复「收到」的人显得过于谄媚，最后回复的人显得不够积极，最安全的策略是在第三到第五个回复。如果老板在群里发了一条工作指令附带一个微笑表情，那个微笑的含义至少有七种可能的解读，跟古代的皇帝圣旨一样需要反复推敲。我有时候觉得，人类花在群聊规则上的认知资源，如果用来学一门外语大概早已精通三种了。
text_pinyin: Wo muqian tongshi cunzai yu liushisan ge weixin qun li, qizhong dayue wushi ge chu yu yongjiu jingyin zhuangtai. Mei ge qun dou you ziji de qian guize, weifan renhe yi tiao dou keneng yinfa shejiao zainan. Biru jiazu qun: zhi neng fa guli xingzhi de neirong he yangsheng wenzhang, juedui bu neng fa zhengzhi guandian huo renhe keneng yinfa zhenglun de huati, fouze ni dayi hui zai xia ci jiating jucan shi yiweishenchang de kan ni yi yan. Tongxue qun de guize geng weimiao: keyi huiyi wangshi dan bu neng ti dangnian shei zhui guo shei, keyi xuanyao haizi dan chengdu bu neng chaoguo zuweihu moren de yuzhì—yiban lai shuo, kao shang zhongdian zhongxue keyi fa, kao shang Qinghua jiu zhi neng diao silian le. Zui fuza de shi gongzuo qun. Laoban fa le yi tiao xiaoxi yihou, di yi ge huifu 'shoudao' de ren xian de guoyu chanmei, zuihou huifu de ren xian de bu gou jiji, zui anquan de celue shi zai di san dao di wu ge huifu. Ruguo laoban zai qun li fa le yi tiao gongzuo zhiling fudai yi ge weixiao biaoqing, nage weixiao de hanyi zhishao you qi zhong keneng de jiedu, gen gudai de huangdi shengzhi yiyang xuyao fanfu tuiqiao. Wo youshihou juede, renlei hua zai qunliao guize shang de renzhi ziyuan, ruguo yong lai xue yi men waiyu dagai zao yi jingtong san zhong le.
text_en: I'm currently a member of sixty-three WeChat groups, about fifty of which are permanently muted. Each group has its own unwritten rules; violating any could trigger a social disaster. For example, the family group: only encouragement and health articles allowed, absolutely no political opinions or controversy-inducing topics, or your aunt will give you a meaningful look at the next family dinner. Classmate group rules are more subtle: reminiscing is fine but don't mention who had a crush on whom back then; you can show off your kids but not beyond the committee's default threshold—getting into a key middle school can be shared, but getting into Tsinghua should be kept to modest private messages. Most complex is the work group. After the boss sends a message, the first to reply 'received' seems too sycophantic, the last to reply seems not proactive enough; the safest strategy is replying third to fifth. If the boss sends a work directive accompanied by a smiley emoji, that smile has at least seven possible interpretations, requiring as much scrutiny as an ancient emperor's imperial decree. I sometimes think that if the cognitive resources humans spend on group chat rules were directed at learning a foreign language, we'd all be fluent in three by now.

title: The Parking Space Diplomacy
text_zh: 我们小区地下车库有九十八个车位和一百零三辆车，这意味着永远有五辆车找不到地方停。这个简单的算术题催生了一套堪比国际外交的复杂博弈。首先是「占位神器」的军备竞赛：最早有人用椅子占车位，很快升级为路锥，再后来出现了写着恶毒警告语的纸板牌、铁链加锁、甚至有人搬来了一盆巨大的仙人掌。物业出了一条规定禁止私人占位，但执行力度约等于联合国决议。于是地下车库形成了一套完全基于人际关系的非正式分配系统：二楼的张总跟保安老李是牌友，他的车位永远有人帮看着；七楼的赵姐每年春节给物业送茶叶，她的车位旁边的过道从来不会被堵住。最精彩的一幕发生在上个月：三楼的刘叔回来发现自己的车位被一辆陌生的外地牌照车占了，他没有打电话投诉，而是默默地把自己的车横着停在那辆车前面，然后回家睡觉了。第二天早上对方车主下来发现出不去，打了十七个电话刘叔都没接。最终对方不得不通过物业、保安、邻居层层传话，亲自上门道歉。刘叔这才不紧不慢地下楼挪车，挪之前还说了句：「记住了，这是我的位置。」——车位外交的最高段位，大概就是不动声色的围困。
text_pinyin: Women xiaoqu dixia cheku you jiushiba ge chewei he yibailingsan liang che, zhe yiweizhe yongyuan you wu liang che zhao bu dao difang ting. Zhege jiandan de suanshu ti cuisheng le yi tao kanbi guoji waijiao de fuza boyi. Shouxian shi 'zhan wei shenqi' de junbei jingsai: zuizao you ren yong yizi zhan chewei, hen kuai shengji wei luzhui, zai houlai chuxian le xie zhe edu jinggaoyu de zhibanpai, tielian jia suo, shenzhi you ren ban lai le yi pen juda de xianrenzhang. Wuye chu le yi tiao guiding jinzhi siren zhan wei, dan zhixing lidu yue dengyu lianheguo jueyi. Yushi dixia cheku xingcheng le yi tao wanquan jiyu renji guanxi de fei zhengshi fenpei xitong: er lou de Zhang zong gen bao'an Lao Li shi paiyou, ta de chewei yongyuan you ren bang kan zhe; qi lou de Zhao jie mei nian chunjie gei wuye song chaye, ta de chewei pangbian de guodao conglai bu hui bei du zhu. Zui jingcai de yi mu fasheng zai shang ge yue: san lou de Liu shu huilai faxian ziji de chewei bei yi liang mosheng de waidi paizhao che zhan le, ta meiyou da dianhua tousu, er shi momo de ba ziji de che heng zhe ting zai na liang che qianmian, ranhou hui jia shuijiao le. Di er tian zaoshang duifang chezhu xialai faxian chu bu qu, da le shiqi ge dianhua Liu shu dou mei jie. Zuizhong duifang budebu tongguo wuye, bao'an, linju cengceng chuanhua, qinzi shangmen daoqian. Liu shu zhe cai bu jin bu man de xialou nuo che, nuo zhiqian hai shuo le ju: 'Jizhu le, zhe shi wo de weizhi.'—chewei waijiao de zuigao duanwei, dagai jiu shi bu dong shengse de weikun.
text_en: Our community's underground garage has ninety-eight spots and one hundred and three cars, meaning five cars can never find parking. This simple arithmetic problem has spawned a complex game rivaling international diplomacy. First came the 'spot-saving device' arms race: it started with chairs, quickly escalated to traffic cones, then appeared cardboard signs with vicious warnings, chains with padlocks, and someone even hauled in a giant cactus. Property management issued a rule banning private spot-claiming, but enforcement was roughly equivalent to UN resolutions. So the underground garage formed a completely relationship-based informal allocation system: Director Zhang on the second floor plays cards with security guard Old Li, so his spot always has someone watching it; Sister Zhao on the seventh floor gives the property staff tea every Chinese New Year, so the aisle beside her spot is never blocked. The most brilliant scene happened last month: Uncle Liu from the third floor returned to find his spot taken by an unfamiliar car with out-of-town plates. He didn't call to complain but silently parked his car sideways in front of the offender, then went home to sleep. The next morning the other driver came down to find they couldn't get out and made seventeen unanswered calls to Uncle Liu. Eventually the driver had to go through property management, security, and neighbors layer by layer to deliver a personal door-to-door apology. Only then did Uncle Liu unhurriedly go down to move his car, saying before he did: 'Remember, this is my spot.'—The highest level of parking diplomacy is probably the siege conducted without a flicker of expression.

title: The Wonton Shop's Last Winter
title_zh: 馄饨店的最后一个冬天
text_zh: 街口那家开了三十年的馄饨店要关了，老板娘周姐说房租涨了三倍，撑不下去了。消息传出来以后的那个星期，店里每天都排长队，平时从不来的人也突然涌了过来——这大概就是人类的本性，只有在即将失去的时候才意识到珍贵。周姐的馄饨没什么惊天动地的秘方，就是猪肉荠菜馅，皮薄到能隐约看见里面的绿色。她包馄饨的速度极快，两只手像在表演魔术，一捏一折一扔，每个馄饨落在笸箩里的声音轻得像一声叹息。我问过她为什么不做外卖扩大销量，她说：「馄饨离开了汤就不是馄饨了。外卖盒里泡二十分钟的馄饨跟我锅里刚捞出来的是两种东西。我卖了三十年，不想在最后几个月毁了自己的名声。」关店那天我特意去吃了最后一碗。周姐照例在碗里放了一撮虾皮、几滴香油、一小把葱花——三十年不变的配置，精确得像一个不需要更新的算法。吃完以后我把碗放在桌上，看着碗底残留的汤汁，忽然觉得这碗馄饨的味道并不特别好吃，但它是这条街的味觉坐标——坐标消失以后，整条街在味觉上就迷路了。
text_pinyin: Jiekou na jia kai le sanshi nian de huntun dian yao guan le, laobanniang Zhou jie shuo fangzu zhang le san bei, cheng bu xia qu le. Xiaoxi chuan chulai yihou de nage xingqi, dian li mei tian dou pai changdui, pingshi cong bu lai de ren ye turan yong le guolai—zhe dagai jiu shi renlei de benxing, zhi you zai jijiang shiqu de shihou cai yishi dao zhengui. Zhou jie de huntun mei shenme jingtian dongdi de mifang, jiu shi zhurou jicai xian, pi bao dao neng yinyue kanjian limian de lvse. Ta bao huntun de sudu ji kuai, liang zhi shou xiang zai biaoyan moshu, yi nie yi zhe yi reng, mei ge huntun luo zai poluo li de shengyin qing de xiang yi sheng tanxi. Wo wen guo ta weishenme bu zuo waimai kuoda xiaoliang, ta shuo: 'Huntun likai le tang jiu bu shi huntun le. Waimai he li pao ershi fenzhong de huntun gen wo guo li gang lao chulai de shi liang zhong dongxi. Wo mai le sanshi nian, bu xiang zai zuihou ji ge yue hui le ziji de mingsheng.' Guan dian na tian wo teyi qu chi le zuihou yi wan. Zhou jie zhaoli zai wan li fang le yi cuo xiapi, ji di xiangyou, yi xiao ba conghua—sanshi nian bu bian de peizhi, jingque de xiang yi ge bu xuyao gengxin de suanfa. Chi wan yihou wo ba wan fang zai zhuo shang, kan zhe wan di canliu de tangzhi, huran juede zhe wan huntun de weidao bing bu tebie hao chi, dan ta shi zhe tiao jie de weijue zuobiao—zuobiao xiaoshi yihou, zheng tiao jie zai weijue shang jiu milu le.
text_en: The wonton shop at the corner that's been open thirty years is closing. The owner, Sister Zhou, says rent tripled and she can't hold on. In the week after the news, the shop had long queues daily; people who never came suddenly appeared—this is probably human nature, only recognizing preciousness when about to lose it. Sister Zhou's wontons have no earth-shattering recipe—just pork and shepherd's purse filling, wrappers thin enough to faintly see the green inside. She wraps wontons incredibly fast, both hands performing magic—one pinch, one fold, one toss, each wonton landing in the basket with a sound light as a sigh. I once asked why she doesn't do delivery to increase sales. She said: 'Wontons without broth aren't wontons. A wonton soaked twenty minutes in a delivery box and one just ladled from my pot are two different things. I've been selling for thirty years; I don't want to ruin my reputation in the last few months.' On closing day I made a point to eat the last bowl. Sister Zhou placed her usual pinch of dried shrimp, few drops of sesame oil, and small handful of scallions in the bowl—thirty years unchanged, precise as an algorithm that never needs updating. After finishing, I set the bowl on the table, looking at the residual broth, and suddenly felt the wonton's flavor wasn't particularly delicious, but it was this street's taste coordinate—once the coordinate disappears, the entire street becomes lost in terms of taste.

title: The Wrong Train
text_zh: 在日本旅行的第三天，我坐错了列车。应该去京都的车把我带到了一个从未听说过的小站，站名用片假名写的，我一个字也看不懂。手机恰好没电了，下一班返程车要等两个小时。我站在空荡荡的站台上，前所未有地感到了一种纯粹的「迷失」——不是焦虑意义上的迷失，而是所有导航和日程都失效以后产生的一种奇异的轻盈感。我走出车站，沿着唯一一条路往前走，路两边是整齐的稻田，远处有几栋灰色的民居，一只猫蹲在邮筒旁边晒太阳。我在一家只有三张桌子的小面馆吃了一碗乌冬面，老板是个不会说英语的老太太，我们之间的交流完全靠手势和微笑。那碗面出奇地好吃，也许是因为饥饿，也许是因为没有任何期待。吃完面我在镇子里走了一圈，发现了一个只有十几块墓碑的小墓地、一家门口贴着手写「定休日」的理发店、和一台还在运转的古老自动贩卖机。这些都不是任何旅行攻略会提到的「景点」，但它们拼凑出了一幅比任何网红打卡地都更真实的日本生活图景。回到站台等车的时候，我想：也许旅行最好的时刻就是计划崩溃的时刻——当你不知道自己在哪里的时候，你才会真正开始看见你所在的地方。
text_pinyin: Zai Riben lxing de di san tian, wo zuo cuo le lieche. Yinggai qu Jingdu de che ba wo dai dao le yi ge cong wei tingshuoguo de xiao zhan, zhanming yong pianjiaming xie de, wo yi ge zi ye kan bu dong. Shouji qiahao mei dian le, xia yi ban fancheng che yao deng liang ge xiaoshi. Wo zhan zai kongdangdang de zhantai shang, qiansuoweiyou de gandao le yi zhong chuncui de 'mishi'—bu shi jiaolv yiyi shang de mishi, er shi suoyou daohang he richeng dou shixi yihou chansheng de yi zhong qiyi de qingying gan. Wo zou chu chezhan, yan zhe weiyi yi tiao lu wang qian zou, lu liang bian shi zhengqi de daotian, yuanchu you ji dong huise de minju, yi zhi mao dun zai youtong pangbian shai taiyang. Wo zai yi jia zhi you san zhang zhuozi de xiao mianguan chi le yi wan wudong mian, laoban shi ge bu hui shuo Yingyu de laotaitai, women zhijian de jiaoliu wanquan kao shoushi he weixiao. Na wan mian chuqi de hao chi, yexu shi yinwei ji'e, yexu shi yinwei meiyou renhe qidai. Chi wan mian wo zai zhenzi li zou le yi quan, faxian le yi ge zhi you shi ji kuai mubei de xiao mudi, yi jia menkou tie zhe shouxie 'dingxiuri' de lifa dian, he yi tai hai zai yunzhuan de gulao zidong fanmai ji. Zhexie dou bu shi renhe lxing gonglue hui ti dao de 'jingdian', dan tamen pincoule yi fu bi renhe wanghong daka di dou geng zhenshi de Riben shenghuo tujing. Hui dao zhantai deng che de shihou, wo xiang: yexu lxing zuihao de shike jiu shi jihua bengkui de shike—dang ni bu zhidao ziji zai nali de shihou, ni cai hui zhenzheng kaishi kanjian ni suozai de difang.
text_en: On the third day of traveling in Japan, I took the wrong train. The one bound for Kyoto took me to a tiny station I'd never heard of, its name written in katakana I couldn't read. My phone happened to be dead, and the next return train was two hours away. Standing on the empty platform, I felt an unprecedented pure 'lostness'—not anxious lostness, but a strange lightness produced when all navigation and schedules become invalid. I walked out of the station along the only road, neat rice paddies on both sides, a few grey houses in the distance, a cat squatting by a mailbox sunbathing. I ate a bowl of udon at a noodle shop with only three tables; the owner was an old lady who spoke no English, and we communicated entirely through gestures and smiles. The noodles were surprisingly delicious—perhaps from hunger, perhaps from having zero expectations. After eating, I walked around the town, discovering a small cemetery with only a dozen headstones, a barbershop with a handwritten 'closed today' sign on its door, and an ancient vending machine still running. None of these are 'sights' any travel guide would mention, but they assembled a picture of Japanese life more authentic than any Instagram hotspot. Waiting for the train back on the platform, I thought: perhaps the best moments of travel are when plans collapse—when you don't know where you are, you finally begin to truly see the place you're in.
